$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Electrons in a cathode ray tube have been emitted with a velocity of 1000 ms-1. The number of following statements which is/are true about the emitted radiation is

Given : h=6×10-34Js,me=9×10-31 kg

(A ) The deBroglie wavelength of the electron emitted is 666.67 nm

(B ) The characteristic of electrons emitted depend upon the material of the electrodes of the cathode ray tube.

(C) The cathode rays start from cathode and move towards anode.

(D ) The nature of the emitted electrons depends on the nature of the gas present in cathode ray tube..
question:
Electrons in a cathode ray tube have been emitted with a velocity of 1000 ms-1. The number of following statements which is/are true about the emitted radiation is

Given : h=6×10-34Js,me=9×10-31 kg

(A ) The deBroglie wavelength of the electron emitted is 666.67 nm

(B ) The characteristic of electrons emitted depend upon the material of the electrodes of the cathode ray tube.

(C) The cathode rays start from cathode and move towards anode.

(D ) The nature of the emitted electrons depends on the nature of the gas present in cathode ray tube..
solution:
(A) Ve=1000 m/s;h=6×10-34Js

me=9×10-31 kg
Debroglie wavelength:

⇒λ=hmv=6×10-349×10-31×1000=666.67×10-9 m

=666.67 nm

(B) The characteristic of electrons emitted is independent of the material of the electrodes of the cathode ray tube. Electrons are fundamental to all traditional forms of matter.

(C) The cathode rays start from cathode and move towards anode. It was discovered by Sir J.J. Thompson.

(D) The nature of the emitted electrons is independent on the nature of the gas present in cathode ray tube. They are fundamental particles.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Two positively charged particles $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{2}$ have been accelerated across the same potential difference of 200 keV as shown below.

[Given mass of $\mathrm{m}_{1}=1$ amu and $\mathrm{m}_{2}=4$ amu]
The deBroglie wavelength of $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ will be $x$ times of $\mathrm{m}_{2}$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ (nearest integer)
question:
Two positively charged particles $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{2}$ have been accelerated across the same potential difference of 200 keV as shown below.

[Given mass of $\mathrm{m}_{1}=1$ amu and $\mathrm{m}_{2}=4$ amu]
The deBroglie wavelength of $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ will be $x$ times of $\mathrm{m}_{2}$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ (nearest integer)
solution:
For a charged particle accelerated through potential V, the de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mVe}}$.
The wavelength is inversely proportional to $\sqrt{m}$.
Therefore: $\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} = \sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{1}} = 2$.
This means $\lambda_1 = 2\lambda_2$, so x = 2 (nearest integer).

subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the correct statements from the following:
A. Heisenberg uncertainty principle is applicable to electrons.
B. The size of $2p_x$ orbital is less than the size of $3p_x$ orbital.
C. The energy of $2s$ orbital of H atom is equal to the energy of $2s$ orbital of Li.
D. The electronic configuration of Cr is $[\text{Ar}] 3d^5 4s^1$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Identify the correct statements from the following:
A. Heisenberg uncertainty principle is applicable to electrons.
B. The size of $2p_x$ orbital is less than the size of $3p_x$ orbital.
C. The energy of $2s$ orbital of H atom is equal to the energy of $2s$ orbital of Li.
D. The electronic configuration of Cr is $[\text{Ar}] 3d^5 4s^1$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Statement A is correct. Heisenberg's uncertainty principle is applicable to microscopic particles such as electrons.

Statement B is correct. The size of an orbital increases with an increase in the principal quantum number $n$. Thus, the size of the $3p_x$ orbital is greater than that of the $2p_x$ orbital.

Statement C is incorrect. The energy of an orbital depends on the effective nuclear charge. Since the nuclear charge of Li ($Z=3$) is greater than that of H ($Z=1$), the energy of the $2s$ orbital of Li is lower than that of the $2s$ orbital of H.

Statement D is correct. Chromium ($Z=24$) has an anomalous electronic configuration of $[\text{Ar}] 3d^5 4s^1$ due to the extra stability of the half-filled $d$-subshell.

Therefore, statements A, B, and D are correct.

Answer: A, B and D Only

subject: Chemistry
q: The calculated spin-only magnetic moments of $\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_6\right]$ and $\mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_6\right]$ respectively are :
question: The calculated spin-only magnetic moments of $\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_6\right]$ and $\mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_6\right]$ respectively are :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_6\right] \\
& \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^5 \\
& \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} \text { with } \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{WFL}) \\
& =\mathrm{t}_{29}{ }^3 \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}{ }^2
\end{aligned}$

Number of unpaired electron ( $n$ ) $=5$
$\mu$ spin only $=5.92 \mathrm{BM}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_6\right] \\
& \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \Rightarrow \mathrm{OH}^{-} \mathrm{(WFL)} \\
& \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^6=\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^4 \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^2 \\
& \mathrm{n}=4
\end{aligned}$
$\mu_{\text {spin only }}=4.90 \mathrm{BM}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The following sets of quantum numbers represent four electrons in an atom.
(i) $n=4, l=1$
(ii) $n=4, l=0$
(iii) $n=3, l=2$
(iv) $n=3, l=1$
The sequence representing increasing order of energy, is
question:
The following sets of quantum numbers represent four electrons in an atom.
(i) $n=4, l=1$
(ii) $n=4, l=0$
(iii) $n=3, l=2$
(iv) $n=3, l=1$
The sequence representing increasing order of energy, is
solution:
(i) $4 p$
(ii) $4 \mathrm{~s}$
(iii) $3 d$
(iv) $3 p$
According to Bohr Bury's $(n+\ell)$ rule, increasing order of energy will be (iv)  < (ii) $ < $ (iii) $ < $ (i).
Note: If the two orbitals have same value of $(n+\ell)$ then the orbital with lower value of $n$ will be filled first.

For Li2+

rL2+n=4-rL2+n=3=0.5293[16-9]=ΔR1

For He+

rHe+n=4-rHe+n=3=0.5292[16-9]=ΔR2

ΔR1ΔR2=23

subject: Chemistry
q: If \(\mathrm{a}_0\) is denoted as the Bohr radius of hydrogen atom, then what is the de-Broglie wavelength \((\lambda)\) of the electron present in the second orbit of hydrogen atom? [n : any integer]
question: If \(\mathrm{a}_0\) is denoted as the Bohr radius of hydrogen atom, then what is the de-Broglie wavelength \((\lambda)\) of the electron present in the second orbit of hydrogen atom? [n : any integer]
solution:
$r_n=\frac{a_0 n^2}{z}$
Also, $2 \pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{n} \lambda$
Where $\lambda$ is de-Broglie wavelength
$\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{a}_0 \mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{z}}=\mathrm{n} \lambda$

For second orbit of H -atom
$\lambda=\frac{8 \pi \mathrm{a}_0}{\mathrm{n}}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Based on Heisenberg's uncertainty principle, the uncertainty in the velocity of the electron to be found within an atomic nucleus of diameter \(10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}\) is ________. \(10^9 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\) (nearest integer)
[Given : mass of electron \(=9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\), Plank's constant \((h)=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\)] (Value of \(\pi=3.14\))
question:
Based on Heisenberg's uncertainty principle, the uncertainty in the velocity of the electron to be found within an atomic nucleus of diameter \(10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}\) is ________. \(10^9 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\) (nearest integer)
[Given : mass of electron \(=9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\), Plank's constant \((h)=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\)] (Value of \(\pi=3.14\))
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m} \Delta \mathrm{V} . \Delta \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{4 \pi} \\ & \begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{V} & =\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^{-15} \times 4 \times 3.14} \\ & =57.97 \times 10^{+9} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The species having identical radii according to the Bohr's theory are:
A. $H$ (first orbit)
B. $He^+$ (first orbit)
C. $He^+$ (Second orbit)
D. $Li^{2+}$ (first orbit)
E. $Be^{3+}$ (Second orbit)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
The species having identical radii according to the Bohr's theory are:
A. $H$ (first orbit)
B. $He^+$ (first orbit)
C. $He^+$ (Second orbit)
D. $Li^{2+}$ (first orbit)
E. $Be^{3+}$ (Second orbit)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
According to Bohr's theory, the radius of the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen-like species is given by $r_n = 0.529 \times \dfrac{n^2}{Z} \text{ \AA}$.

For $H$ (first orbit), $n = 1$, $Z = 1 \Rightarrow r = 0.529 \times \dfrac{1^2}{1} = 0.529 \text{ \AA}$.

For $He^+$ (first orbit), $n = 1$, $Z = 2 \Rightarrow r = 0.529 \times \dfrac{1^2}{2} = 0.2645 \text{ \AA}$.

For $He^+$ (second orbit), $n = 2$, $Z = 2 \Rightarrow r = 0.529 \times \dfrac{2^2}{2} = 1.058 \text{ \AA}$.

For $Li^{2+}$ (first orbit), $n = 1$, $Z = 3 \Rightarrow r = 0.529 \times \dfrac{1^2}{3} = 0.176 \text{ \AA}$.

For $Be^{3+}$ (second orbit), $n = 2$, $Z = 4 \Rightarrow r = 0.529 \times \dfrac{2^2}{4} = 0.529 \text{ \AA}$.

The radii of $H$ (first orbit) and $Be^{3+}$ (second orbit) are identical. Therefore, species A and E have identical radii.

Answer: A and E Only

subject: Chemistry
q:
The minimum uncertainty in the speed of an electron in one dimensional region of length 2ao
(Where ao= Bohr radius 52.9pm) is____kms-1 (Nearest integer)  (Given : Mass of electron =9.1×10-31 kg, Planck's constant h=6.63×10-34Js)
question:
The minimum uncertainty in the speed of an electron in one dimensional region of length 2ao
(Where ao= Bohr radius 52.9pm) is____kms-1 (Nearest integer)  (Given : Mass of electron =9.1×10-31 kg, Planck's constant h=6.63×10-34Js)
solution:
Heisenberg's uncertainty principle

Δx×Δpx≥h4π

⇒2a0×mΔvx=h4π(minimum)

⇒Δvx=h4π×12a0×1 m

=6.63×10-344×3.14×2×52.9×10-12×9.1×10-31

=548273 ms-1

=548.273 km s-1

=548 km s-1

subject: Chemistry
q:
The electronic configuration for Neodymium is:

[Atomic Number for Neodymium 60]
question:
The electronic configuration for Neodymium is:

[Atomic Number for Neodymium 60]
solution:
The atomic number of neodymium is 60.

Therefore, the electronic configuration of neodymium (Nd) can be determined by distributing its electrons according to the Aufbau principle, Pauli exclusion principle, and Hund's rule.

Electronic configuration of Nd(Z = 60) is;

[Xe] 4f46s2

as the last electron of Neodymium enters the f subshell. so it is called a f-block element.

Hence option A is the answer.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following point in Figure 2 most accurately represents the nodal surface as shown in Figure 1?
question: Which of the following point in Figure 2 most accurately represents the nodal surface as shown in Figure 1?
solution:
The 2s orbital has one radial nodal surface, which is a spherical surface shown in Figure 1.
A nodal surface is where the electron probability density (and wave function) equals zero, meaning $\psi_{2s}(x) = 0$.
From the wave function graph in Figure 2, point B represents where the wave function crosses zero on the x-axis, indicating the location of the radial node.
Points A and C show maximum and minimum values respectively, while point D shows a non-zero region beyond the node.

subject: Chemistry
q: Ejection of the photoelectron from metal in the photoelectric effect experiment can be stopped by applying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, when the radiation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> is used. The work function of the metal is
question: Ejection of the photoelectron from metal in the photoelectric effect experiment can be stopped by applying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, when the radiation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> is used. The work function of the metal is
solution:
λ=250 nm=2500 A 

E=hcλ=124002500=4.96 eV

KE=e×stopping potential=0.5 eV

E=W0+KE

4.96=Wo + 0.5

W0=4.46≈4.5 eV

subject: Chemistry
q:
A ball weighing 10 g is moving with a velocity of 90 m s-1. If the uncertainty in its velocity is 5%, then the uncertainty in its position is _  ×10-33 m. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given: h=6.63×10-34 Js]
question:
A ball weighing 10 g is moving with a velocity of 90 m s-1. If the uncertainty in its velocity is 5%, then the uncertainty in its position is _  ×10-33 m. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given: h=6.63×10-34 Js]
solution:
Δv=90×5100

=4.5 m s-1

Δv.Δx=h4πm

Δx=h4πm·Δv

=6.63×10-344×3.14×0.01×4.5

=1.17×10-33

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following statements are true?
(A) The subsidiary quantum number $l$ describes the shape of the orbital occupied by the electron.
(B)

is the boundary surface diagram of the $2 p_x$ orbital.
(C) The + and - signs in the wave function of the $2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}$ orbital refer to charge.
(D) The wave function of $2 p_x$ orbital is zero everywhere in the xy plane.
question:
Which of the following statements are true?
(A) The subsidiary quantum number $l$ describes the shape of the orbital occupied by the electron.
(B)

is the boundary surface diagram of the $2 p_x$ orbital.
(C) The + and - signs in the wave function of the $2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}$ orbital refer to charge.
(D) The wave function of $2 p_x$ orbital is zero everywhere in the xy plane.
solution:
(A) Azimuthal quantum number $(\ell)$ indicates the shape of orbital occupied by the electron
(B)

$\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}$ orbital
(C) The + and - sign in the wave function of $2 p_x$ orbital refer to the sign (Phase) of the wave function, not the charge
(D) The wave function of $2 p_x$ orbital will be zero in yz plane (Nodal plane).

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following sets of quantum numbers is correct for an electron in $4 \mathrm{f}$ orbital?
question: Which of the following sets of quantum numbers is correct for an electron in $4 \mathrm{f}$ orbital?
solution:
$4 \mathrm{f} \longrightarrow \mathrm{n}=4$
$\mathrm{l}=3$
$m=-\mathrm{l}$ to $+1$
$-3$ to $+3$
